_about.status: v2 — WEEKLY report only, restructured by report_kind for the monthly corpus (migration 010). This file is the corpus of the weekly/monthly report engine ONLY: yearly is a Vimshottari composition over dasha_content, and never flows through here.
_about.axis: per report_kind: shape (6, a DISTRIBUTION claim not a trend) x opening variant (17) | turn kind (5) x 7 | area.role (18) x 5 | close by shape (6) x 5
_about.counts: weekly: 102 openings + 35 turns + 90 standings + 30 closes = 257 authored lines
_about.why_primes: 17, 7 and 5 are mutually coprime and none shares a factor with 52. A rotation whose period divides the report cadence collapses — a 12-row table indexed by month hands every January the same row forever, and 13 is the same trap one door down: 13 divides 52, so a 13-slot rotation would hand every 52-week anniversary the same opening. 17 is the smallest count above 12 that clears 52, 7 and 5 at once.
_about.why_v2: v1's 11 openings guaranteed 11 distinct consecutive reports, so reports #1 and #12 could draw the same opening cell — visible whenever both weeks shared a shape, which is common (scattered is half of real weeks). 17 stretches the guarantee to 17 consecutive reports and the full-skeleton recurrence to lcm(17,7,5) = 595 weeks. The six new lines per shape are APPENDED after the original 11 so v1-indexed code draws the same line for the same index.
_about.why_distribution: The first draft classified weeks as rising/falling/cresting/dipping. Real sky data produced only two of those six, because daily energy is a tara-driven sawtooth (nine alternating energies, advancing one step per day) with a 48-70 point weekly spread and no trend at all. These six describe WHERE the strong days fall, which is a claim the data actually supports.

openings: This week is level. Nothing distinguishes one day from another, which makes it excellent for anything requiring consistency.
An even seven days. No day to aim at and none to avoid, so the schedule can be whatever you decide.
It holds the same note throughout. Routine tasks, long projects and anything needing repetition all suit this shape.
There is no rhythm here worth planning around. That is not emptiness; it is the absence of interference.
A plain week. Nothing argues with you in either direction, so your own discipline sets the pace.
Every day sits at one level. Weeks like this are where backlogs actually get cleared.
No standout days in either direction. The differences are smaller than your own mood will be.
It runs flat. Pick the project that benefits from a long uninterrupted run and give it all seven days.
This one does not move much. Treat the whole as a single block rather than as parts.
Uniform throughout. What you get out of it depends almost entirely on what you put in.
A quiet, unvarying week. Nothing will carry you and nothing will obstruct you.
Seven days of the same weather. Choose what deserves a steady run and give it the whole stretch.
Nothing here rewards timing. Effort is the only variable left, which at least makes the arithmetic simple.
The week neither helps nor hinders. Progress will look exactly like whatever you repeated.
No peaks to catch and no dips to dodge. Set a pace you can hold for seven days and hold it.
Level from Monday to Sunday. If you have been waiting for a neutral week to begin a routine, this is it.
One flat line all the way across. Judge your days by what got finished, because the sky will not rank them.
Your strongest days land at the front. Whatever needs sharp judgement should be scheduled before midweek, not after.
The week gives most of what it has early. Treat the opening as prime time rather than as a warm-up.
Front-loaded. The two highest days arrive before most people have finished planning, so do your planning on Sunday.
The strong days cluster at the start. That is convenient if you act on it and wasteful if you do not.
This week opens at its best. Nothing later matches those first days, so spend them rather than saving them.
The high days come early. Anything waiting for the right moment has its moment almost immediately.
The first half holds most of it. Book the hard conversations there and let the rest be admin.
Early days carry this one. By the back half you will be working on what you set up, not setting up anything new.
The opening days are the ones that count. Judge the week by what you do in them.
Strength arrives immediately and does not wait around. Move the important thing forward in your calendar.
The best of this week is at the beginning. Read that as an instruction about scheduling, not about luck.
The week pays out in advance. Take the difficult conversation early and coast on the result.
Monday and Tuesday do the heavy lifting here. Arrive with the list already written.
What this week gives, it gives first. Waiting costs more than usual, so decide quickly and act on it.
The opening carries the value. Spend it on decisions, because the later days suit routine better.
Best days first, quieter days after. Sequence the hard work accordingly and the whole week feels generous.
By Wednesday the best of it has been given. Whatever needs sharp judgement should not sit late in the calendar.
Your strongest days are at the end. Push what matters later rather than forcing it early.
The week saves its strongest for last. The opening days are for preparation and they are genuinely enough for that.
Back-loaded. Whatever feels heavy at the start gets noticeably lighter once the strong days arrive.
The high days cluster near the close. Protect them — do not let routine fill the end of your week.
Most of the strength sits in the second half. Move deadlines back where you honestly can.
This one finishes better than it starts. Do not judge it on Monday.
The capable days come late. Anything requiring sharp judgement belongs at the far end.
Strength arrives toward the finish. Use the opening to clear the ground so the strong days are free.
The closing days are the ones that count. Everything before them is legitimately preparation.
This week's peak is at the end. Arrange things so you are not too tired to use it.
Later is stronger. Every task you can honestly defer gets better support when you defer it.
The capable days hold back until the finish. Clear the ground beforehand so they arrive unencumbered.
A slow open and a sound finish. Put the decisions late and the paperwork early.
What matters lands late this week. Resist forcing it ahead of time; the support is simply not there yet.
The far half holds the good days. Aim anything that needs judgement there and keep the opening light.
The last days are the capable ones. Until they arrive, preparation is the honest work.
Nothing about a slow opening is a verdict. The days that decide this week all sit in its second half.
This week is strong at both ends and thin through the middle. Book the demanding things either side of the centre.
Two good runs with a gap between them. The gap is short and it does not need managing.
The strength sits at the edges. Midweek is for maintenance, and there is no shame in planning it that way.
Both ends are better than the centre. Plan two pushes rather than one long one.
You get a strong opening and a strong close. What sits between them is quieter than either.
The middle is the soft spot. Schedule around it and this reads as two solid runs rather than one uneven week.
Energy comes in two blocks. Treat them as separate weeks and the middle stops being a problem.
Strong, thin, strong. Knowing the order in advance is most of what you need.
The centre asks least of you. Let it, and put the real effort on either side.
This one has a dip in the middle with strength around it. The dip is bounded, which is the useful bit.
The edges carry this week. Midweek is for whatever tolerates a slower pace.
Strong edges and a quiet middle. Two short campaigns with a rest between them is the natural plan.
The week works in bookends. Open hard, close hard, and let midweek be deliberately unambitious.
Patience is for the days between the pushes. Both ends will repay whatever the centre defers.
A lull sits between two able runs. Schedule the lull on purpose and it stops being one.
Two working stretches with a valley in the middle. Neither is long, so neither should be wasted.
Begin firmly, ease off, finish firmly. The soft centre is part of the design, not a failure of it.
Your strongest days sit in the middle. Aim the thing that matters at midweek rather than at either edge.
The centre carries this one. Both ends are ordinary; what sits between them is not.
Midweek is where the strength is. Use the opening to prepare and the close to consolidate.
The high days are central. That gives you a run-up and a wind-down, which is the most workable arrangement there is.
Neither end of this week tells you much. The middle is where it actually happens.
Strength gathers toward the centre. Book the demanding conversation for midweek and it will go better.
Midweek holds the peak. Do not spend yourself on the opening trying to get ahead.
This one peaks between its ends. Approach that deliberately rather than arriving already tired.
The centre is the strong stretch. Front and back are for setup and tidying respectively.
Midweek does the heavy lifting. Everything else is scaffolding around it.
The best days are neither first nor last. Plan around the middle.
Wednesday to Friday is where the capacity sits. Treat the opening as a ramp and the close as a landing.
This week bulges in the middle. Put the one thing that matters most exactly there.
The edges are ordinary and the centre is not. A push at the halfway mark will get further than an early one.
Capacity peaks midweek and tapers on both sides. Match the workload to that outline and nothing is wasted.
The high days sit shoulder to shoulder in the centre. One sustained effort there beats two half-efforts at the edges.
Open light, peak in the middle, land soft. Weeks built this way reward the patient opener.
The strong days are scattered rather than clustered. Check each morning instead of planning a run.
Capable and thin days alternate. That is workable, but it rewards a flexible schedule over a fixed one.
This week has no block of strength — it comes a day at a time, with gaps between.
Strength arrives in single days rather than runs. Keep the demanding tasks short enough to fit one.
The good days do not sit together. Pair each with a lighter one after it and the week runs evenly.
It alternates. Expect to feel capable and then ordinary in quick succession, and read nothing into either.
No two strong days in a row. Plan the important things one at a time rather than in a push.
The week comes in single high days. That suits errands and decisions better than long projects.
Strength is distributed rather than concentrated. There is no standout run here, only standout days.
The rhythm is uneven. Take the strong days as they come and keep everything else light.
This one gives and withholds by turns. Short commitments will serve you better than long ones.
The strength comes interleaved with ordinary days. Match each task to the day it deserves, morning by morning.
No run of good days this week, only good days singly. Size the work so one day can complete it.
The week deals its cards one at a time. Treat each one as a separate opportunity rather than the start of something.
Good days arrive without warning and leave the same way. A loose plan will outperform a rigid one.
Nothing clusters this week. Alternate the demanding work with the routine and the rhythm takes care of itself.
Expect single good days with plain ones between. Keep commitments day-sized and the unevenness never costs you.

lines: The strongest hours arrive almost immediately, so the decision worth making is what you point them at.
Your high lands near the start, which gives you everything after it to build on.
The turn happens early. Past it the week is not weaker so much as differently shaped.
Peak hours come before most people have settled in. Be ready rather than warmed up.
It opens near its best. Waiting for a better moment means waiting past it.
The high sits at the front. Everything later is downstream of what you do there.
Early is when this week is at its most capable. Treat the first hours as the main event.
The pivot is in the middle. What happens there sets the tone for everything after.
Your strongest hours sit at the centre, with room on both sides to prepare and recover.
The turn comes midweek. Approach it deliberately rather than arriving at it already spent.
The middle carries the high. Book the thing you have been putting off against it.
Conditions top out in the centre. Neither end is where the leverage is.
The hinge is midweek. Before it is preparation; after it is consequence.
Peak sits between the ends. That is the most workable position it can take.
The strongest conditions arrive at the end. Everything before them is legitimately preparation.
Your high is late, so resist judging the week before it has finished.
The turn comes near the close. Save what needs strength for it rather than spending early.
Everything peaks in the final hours. That is worth protecting against fatigue.
The strongest hours are the last ones. Arrange things so you can still use them.
Late is when this week is at its best. Do not fill the ending with tidying.
The high lands at the finish. Whatever you have been building meets its best moment there.
There is no pivot to plan around. That is genuinely useful information rather than a gap.
Nothing turns. The conditions you get on the first morning are the conditions you get throughout.
No single moment carries more weight than another. Your schedule can ignore the sky entirely.
There is no best hour to wait for. Start when you are ready and the week will cooperate.
It does not hinge anywhere. Consistency, not timing, is what will move things.
No turn, no peak, no dip. Pick your own moment and it will be as good as any other.
Nothing asks for particular timing. That freedom is the shape.
Your best and worst days are next to each other. The drop is real but it is one day wide.
The high and the low sit back to back. Do not read the weaker one as a trend.
There is a sharp change between two consecutive days. Knowing which order they come in is most of the advantage.
The strongest and thinnest days are neighbours. Plan nothing important across both.
You will go from capable to ordinary overnight, or the reverse. Neither says anything about the week as a whole.
Two adjacent days differ more than the rest combined. Let the weaker one be genuinely light.
The swing between consecutive days is the sharpest thing here. Expect it and it costs nothing.
Love carries the week. Conversations land where you aim them, so say the thing you have been circling.
Closeness comes easily. It is a good week to ask for what you actually want from someone.
The people who matter are reachable. Warmth carries further than usual, and reaching out costs you very little.
Relationships lead. Time spent with someone is worth more now than the same time spent alone.
Your closest connections are where the generosity is. Use it on the relationship you have been neglecting.
Love asks for patience. Nothing is wrong, but conversations need more room than you would normally give them.
Closeness moves slowly now. Let misreadings pass rather than correcting them, and they will settle by themselves.
Relationships want less pressure. A postponed conversation is not an avoided one.
The people around you are harder to read. Ask rather than assume, and expect the answer to take a while.
Connection runs thin. Keep expectations small and you will not be disappointed.
Love is reliable throughout. Whatever else moves, the people close to you stay where you left them.
Relationships hold level. That makes them the right place to rest when everything else is uneven.
Closeness neither peaks nor dips. It is the fixed point you can plan around.
Your connections are the constant here. Nothing asks anything unusual of them.
The people close to you do not shift. Lean there when everything else moves.
Money decisions resolve cleanly. Comparisons make sense and the sensible option is visibly the sensible one.
Your accounts are unusually legible. Good timing for the purchase you researched, not the one you just thought of.
Asks land well. If there is a rate to negotiate or a bill to query, do it now.
Spending decisions come easy — not because more is arriving, but because you can see the trade-offs properly.
Money is where the clarity sits. Review the subscriptions, settle the dues, sign what has been waiting.
Money wants checking rather than committing. Hold the big swing and the small ones will still be fine.
Decisions about spending are murkier than usual. A second opinion is worth more than your first instinct.
Nothing about the accounts is urgent. Routine payments are safe; anything requiring a signature can wait.
Comparisons will not resolve cleanly. Sleep on the ones that matter and revisit them later.
Money asks for the slower hand. Reviewing where it went beats deciding where it goes next.
Money holds level. No decision demands anything of you, which is a reasonable argument for simply not spending.
The accounts stay flat. Neither pressure nor opportunity, so the routine can just run.
Spending sits exactly where it did. What worked before works now, unchanged.
Money neither helps nor hinders. Set it aside and give the attention to whatever is actually moving.
Your finances are quiet. Nothing here needs a decision from you.
Work is where the most is given. Push the thing that has been waiting for a good moment — this is it.
Your professional standing rises. Visibility comes cheap, so let the right people see what you ship.
Career leads. Ship what is nearly finished rather than starting something that will not land in time.
The workplace cooperates. Ask for the thing you need from the person who can grant it.
Work runs ahead of everything else. Take on the piece slightly beyond you; conditions will cover the gap.
Work wants consolidation rather than expansion. Finish before you start.
Professionally, everything moves at half pace. Deliver what is promised and resist adding to the pile.
Career is what resists. Nothing is going wrong; it simply does not reward pushing.
The workplace is harder going. Keep commitments modest and they will all be met.
The job asks for less ambition and more follow-through. That trade is worth making.
Work holds its level. The same effort produces the same result on any morning.
Your professional life stays exactly level. Predictability suits long tasks, so give it one.
Career neither climbs nor slips. Use the flatness for anything needing uninterrupted attention.
The workplace is unremarkable, which is the ideal condition for a backlog.
Your standing stays where it is. Nothing will move it for you, and nothing will move against you.
Thinking is sharp. The problem you have been avoiding because it is complicated is the one to open.
Your attention is worth more than usual. Give it to something deserving the quality it has right now.
Clarity leads. Decisions sitting unresolved will resolve if you sit with them properly.
The mind runs clean. Reading, planning and anything requiring sustained thought all go well.
Focus is available. Protect a long block for the task only good concentration can finish.
Thinking comes slower. Write decisions down rather than trusting you will hold them.
Attention scatters more easily. Shorten the blocks and the work still gets done.
Clarity comes at a cost. Reduce what you are holding at once and the remainder gets sharper.
The mind resists complexity. Save the intricate problem for a better week.
Concentration runs thin. Single tasks, one at a time, with the notifications off.
Thinking holds level — neither brilliant nor foggy, but reliably adequate, which is what most days need.
Attention is the constant. Whatever you could concentrate on before, you can concentrate on now.
The mind stays even. Consistency here is worth more than a peak you have to catch.
Clarity neither improves nor slips. Plan on ordinary thinking and you will not be caught out.
Your focus is dependable throughout. Build the schedule on it.
The body is willing. Good timing to restart something physical you let lapse.
Energy in the body runs high. Movement will feel like less effort than it did recently.
Health leads. Sleep, food and exercise all cooperate, so the habit you want to build has room.
Your physical condition is where the surplus is. Spend some of it rather than banking all of it.
The body cooperates. Push the pace slightly and it will hold.
The body wants rest. That is a scheduling instruction rather than a warning.
Physical energy is genuinely lower. Lighter sessions, earlier nights, and everything goes fine.
Health asks for maintenance rather than gains. Hold the routine and do not add to it.
The body is slower to recover. Build in the gap rather than pushing through it.
Rest is the productive choice. Treat sleep as the task, not the leftover.
The body holds level. No surge and no slump, so the routine you have will simply keep working.
Physical condition is the constant. Nothing here needs adjusting.
Health neither improves nor demands. Keep doing what you were doing.
Your body is entirely predictable. That makes it an easy week for building a habit.
Physical energy stays flat. Consistency, not intensity, is what gets rewarded.
Your mood runs generous. Whatever else happens, how you feel about it stays warm.
Inner weather is bright. Use the buoyancy on something that usually feels heavy.
Mood leads. Ideal timing for the thing requiring you to be in a settled state before you start.
You will feel more like yourself than usual. Spend it on people or work that benefit from that.
The emotional weather is the surplus. It will carry the parts that are thinner.
Mood asks the most of you. Lower what you expect of yourself and everything improves immediately.
Inner weather is unsettled. Do not make permanent decisions from a temporary state.
How you feel will lag behind how things actually are. Check the facts before trusting the feeling.
The emotional weather runs heavier. Fewer commitments, more room, and it passes.
Mood lags behind everything else. Be as patient with yourself as you would be with anyone else.
Mood holds level — neither high nor low, which makes it a reliable base for everything else.
Inner weather is the constant. Nothing will swing how you feel.
How you feel stays even. That evenness is worth using on something needing a calm hand.
The emotional weather is unchanging. Plan on it and you will be right.
Your mood is the fixed point. Let the rest move around it.
A run like this is measured by what you put into it. Look at the output, not the sky.
Close by acknowledging the volume. Level weeks clear more than dramatic ones and get no credit.
Nothing about the rhythm decided this. Whatever happened, you did it.
Finish and start the next one the same way. Continuity is this shape's only real advantage.
End with the routine intact. That is the entire point of seven days that do not move.
Carry the early gains forward. What worked in the first days is what to repeat.
Bank what the strong days produced and do not measure everything by a quiet ending.
Close by tidying rather than starting. New things belong on the other side of Sunday.
The tail is for consolidation. Leave it consolidated and Monday opens clean.
Stop before you are forced to. Ending deliberately beats ending exhausted.
End strong and leave it strong. Monday starts from where this finished.
The gain is real — write down what changed so you can repeat it.
What you built in the last days compounds. Do not undo it with a slow restart.
Finish while the good days are still with you, then stop. The stopping matters too.
Close on the upswing and let the slow opening be forgotten.
You end where you started, which after a thin middle is a genuinely good outcome.
Two good runs is more than most weeks give. Count them separately.
The recovery after the centre is the story. Note that it happened without intervention.
Finish knowing the soft patch was bounded. That is worth remembering next time one arrives.
Judge this week from its edges, not from what sat between them.
Come down deliberately. The descent is part of the rhythm and it deserves a plan.
Consolidate after the strong days rather than chasing them. What midweek gained needs securing.
The peak days have passed by the close — that is expected, not a loss.
Use the tail to finish what the centre started. Nothing new belongs there.
End by writing down what the peak produced. It will not seem obvious later.
Do not draw conclusions from a week this uneven. Wait for a calmer one to decide anything permanent.
Close by writing down what actually happened. The impression will be less accurate than the record.
Let it end without a verdict. Alternating weeks resist summary, and forcing one misleads.
Finish by simplifying. Fewer open threads is the right outcome here.
End with slack still in the schedule. That is the win, not any particular achievement.
